title: Tachypnea After C-Section - TTN
chief_complaint: Full-term male born via elective C-section (no labor) at 39 weeks. Tachypnea (RR 80) and mild retractions at 2 hours of life. SpO2 93% on room air. Otherwise active.

physical_exam.chest: Tachypnea with mild subcostal retractions, clear breath sounds, no grunting

hidden_data.cxr: Prominent perihilar streaking, fluid in fissures, hyperinflation

key_learning_points: C-section without labor = risk factor (no thoracic squeeze)
CXR: fluid in fissures + perihilar streaking

patient_responses.breathing: He's breathing fast but seems comfortable
patient_responses.feeding: He tried to breastfeed but got tired quickly

feedback.correct_message: Well diagnosed! TTN is self-limited and resolves within 48h.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is TTN. The C-section + CXR findings are classic.

cxr.comment: Shows perihilar streaking and fissure fluid.

feedback.attending_note: TTN management: Oxygen to maintain SpO2 >92%, monitor respiratory rate and work of breathing. Most resolve within 24-48h. IV fluids if tachypnea >80 (risk of aspiration). Reassure parents - this is benign and self-limited.
